items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: Does Aquinas's Third Way claim the universe had a beginning in time?
items.correct: No — it argues from contingency: even an eternal series of contingent things would depend on a necessary being; Aquinas thought reason alone cannot prove the world began.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Third Way is the claim that the Big Bang shows the universe began and so needs a cause.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Third Way argues from the definition of God to his existence.
items.why: Temporal beginning belongs to the Kalam argument; Aquinas's concern is dependence, not chronology. He explicitly held creation's temporal beginning is known by revelation, not demonstration.

items.q: Does the question 'What caused God?' refute the cosmological argument?
items.correct: Not as it stands — the argument's premise concerns contingent or caused things; God is posited as a necessary being, so the premise does not apply to God without further argument.
items.misconception: Asking 'What caused God?' instantly refutes the cosmological argument because everything must have a cause.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument says God caused himself.
items.why: The retort works only against the strawman premise 'everything has a cause'. Serious critique instead questions whether 'necessary being' is coherent (Hume) or whether the whole needs explaining (Russell).

items.q: Which of Aquinas's Five Ways argues from contingency?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Third Way. “First Way” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Principle of Sufficient Reason is associated with:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Leibniz. “Aquinas” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many 'ways' does Aquinas present in ST I Q.2 a.3?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
